name: Pillars of Creation
introduction: You're looking at stellar nurseries 6,500 light-years away — so far that the light reaching your eyes right now left before ancient Rome fell. These towering columns of gas and dust are the Pillars of Creation, sculpted by the fierce ultraviolet radiation of newborn stars blazing just out of frame. Each pillar stretches several light-years tall; the tallest would dwarf the distance between our Sun and its nearest stellar neighbor. Inside these dark, opaque fingers, new solar systems are quietly assembling themselves from the same raw ingredients that once made you. Webb's infrared eyes pierce through dust that was opaque to Hubble, revealing hundreds of embryonic stars embedded in the columns' walls — orange-red specks caught in the act of ignition. This is not destruction you're witnessing, but creation: an ongoing stellar maternity ward that has been producing stars for millions of years. Explore the hotspots to zoom into individual pillars and meet some of the youngest suns in our galaxy.

hotspots.label: The Pillar Tips
hotspots.description: At the tips of each pillar, ultraviolet radiation from off-frame young stars is slowly evaporating the gas — a process astronomers call photoevaporation. The bright, hazy edges glow because hydrogen is being ionized at the boundary. Yet this same erosion triggers new star formation: the pressure wave drives gas inward, seeding the next generation. Destruction and creation happening simultaneously. What does it tell us that stars are born from the death of their own nurseries?

hotspots.label: Embryonic Stars
hotspots.description: Those ruddy orange-red specks scattered along the pillar walls are protostars — objects that have collapsed from gas but haven't yet begun nuclear fusion. They're surrounded by spinning disks of gas and dust that may one day form planetary systems. Webb detected hundreds of these embryonic stars that Hubble couldn't see through the dust. Each orange speck you see here is a potential solar system in waiting. How many Earth-like worlds might already be forming inside this single column?

hotspots.label: EGGs — Evaporating Gaseous Globules
hotspots.description: The finger-like protrusions along the pillar edges are EGGs — Evaporating Gaseous Globules — dense knots of gas dense enough to resist photoevaporation while everything around them gets stripped away. Each one is a stellar embryo wrapped in its own protective cocoon. As the outer gas burns off, the EGG is exposed — and if a star has already started forming inside, it appears as a bright point at the tip of the protrusion. You're watching stars unwrap themselves.

hotspots.label: Newborn Star Cluster
hotspots.description: The scattered blue and white points in the upper portions of the image are young, massive stars — the same ones whose radiation is sculpting the pillars below. Some are only a few million years old, practically newborns by stellar standards. Their fierce radiation is simultaneously destroying the nebula that birthed them and triggering new star formation deeper inside. Massive stars like these live fast and die young, eventually exploding as supernovae that will scatter the material for the next cycle of creation.

scaleTranslations: The tallest pillar stretches 4 light-years from base to tip — roughly the same distance as from our Sun to its nearest neighboring star, Proxima Centauri.
Each of those embryonic stars is surrounded by a protoplanetary disk potentially wider than our entire solar system, made of the same dust and gas that formed Earth 4.5 billion years ago.
The pillars have been slowly eroding under stellar radiation for 100,000 years — a cosmic blink, yet ten times longer than all of recorded human history.
